examples.title: Basic Linear Search
examples.description: Standard implementation that returns the index of target or -1 if not found
examples.explanation: Iterates through each element using index. Compares each element with target. Returns immediately when found, or -1 after checking all elements.

examples.title: Pythonic Version with enumerate
examples.description: More readable Python implementation using enumerate
examples.explanation: enumerate() provides both index and value in each iteration, making the code more readable and Pythonic.

examples.title: Sentinel Linear Search
examples.description: Optimized version that eliminates bounds checking in loop
examples.code:
def sentinel_search(arr, target):
 """Sentinel search - faster by avoiding bounds check."""
 n = len(arr)
 if n == 0:
 return -1
 
 # Save last element and place sentinel
 last = arr[n - 1]
 arr[n - 1] = target
 
 i = 0
 while arr[i] != target:
 i += 1
 
 # Restore original last element
 arr[n - 1] = last
 
 # Check if found before sentinel position
 if i < n - 1 or arr[n - 1] == target:
 return i
 return -1
examples.explanation: By placing the target at the end (sentinel), we guarantee the loop will terminate without checking bounds. This removes one comparison per iteration.

examples.title: Find All Occurrences
examples.description: Returns all indices where target appears
examples.code:
def find_all(arr, target):
 """Find all indices where target appears."""
 indices = []
 for i, value in enumerate(arr):
 if value == target:
 indices.append(i)
 return indices

# Example
numbers = [1, 4, 2, 4, 3, 4, 5]
result = find_all(numbers, 4)
print(f"Found at indices: {result}") # Output: [1, 3, 5]
examples.explanation: Instead of returning on first match, collects all matching indices in a list. Useful when duplicates exist and all positions are needed.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of matches

examples.title: Java Implementation
examples.description: Linear search in Java with generics
examples.explanation: Generic implementation works with any object type. Uses equals() for comparison instead of == to properly compare object values.

examples.title: C Implementation
examples.explanation: C requires passing array size separately since arrays decay to pointers. Uses simple for loop and direct comparison.
